item.title: PNG Aviation spared from global jet fuel crisis, watchdog confirms sufficient stock
item.description: While global airlines slash daily flight schedules, spike airfares, and seek massive emergency bailouts amid a highly volatile international aviation fuel crisis, Papua New Guinea’s domestic aviation industry remains remarkably insulated. The Independent Consumer and Competition Commission [ICCC] has confirmed that the country currently holds sufficient stockpiles of Jet A1 fuel, successfully aver
item.content:encoded:
While global airlines slash daily flight schedules, spike airfares, and seek massive emergency bailouts amid a highly volatile international aviation fuel crisis, Papua New Guinea’s domestic aviation industry remains remarkably insulated.

The Independent Consumer and Competition Commission [ICCC] has confirmed that the country currently holds sufficient stockpiles of Jet A1 fuel, successfully averting a potential transport crisis that could have paralyzed the national economy.

The stabilization brings critical relief to a nation heavily reliant on regional air networks. Because the Papua New Guinea mainland is largely road-locked and geographically isolated from scattered island provinces, air transport serves as an absolute lifeline, connecting millions of citizens and cargo routes to the capital, Port Moresby. Any sudden shortage of aviation fuel among domestic operators would trigger a immediate, devastating ripple effect across domestic commerce, public travel, and medical supply chains.

"In terms of our consultations with relevant stakeholders and also industry players, we have been informed that for Jet A1 fuel, there is enough stock in the market," ICCC Commissioner Roy Daggy stated in response to questions from NBC News in Port Moresby on Tuesday.

"So there is no challenge… fuel distributors say there are enough fuel stocks in the market," the head of the consumer and competition watchdog said.

While Commissioner Daggy stopped short of disclosing the exact duration of the country's current aviation fuel reserves, the regulatory assurance provides major breathing room for local commercial operators. The ongoing global fuel crunch has been fueled by limited supply on the international market, a continued aftershock of escalating conflicts in the Middle East.

Papua New Guinea's domestic shield against these imported price shocks is backed by direct government intervention. The ICCC sits as a core member of the High-Level State Taskforce, which was established by the National Government to implement its aggressive fiscal policies. The taskforce remains actively engaged in subsidizing fuel prices to cushion the domestic economy from the inflationary impacts of refined petroleum imports.

item.title: Solar lights boost church services in Nipa Kutubu
item.description: Pastors in remote parts of Nembi Plateau LLG are set to improve night services and prepare sermons after receiving solar lights from community leader Fiebik Kilip. The presentations were done at Topua Village. For years, congregations across the area have struggled to hold evening fellowships and worship services due to the lack of electricity. Many pastors relied on candles and small torches to c
item.content:encoded:
Pastors in remote parts of Nembi Plateau LLG are set to improve night services and prepare sermons after receiving solar lights from community leader Fiebik Kilip. The presentations were done at Topua Village.

For years, congregations across the area have struggled to hold evening fellowships and worship services due to the lack of electricity. Many pastors relied on candles and small torches to continue preaching.

Kilip, the former Southern Highlands Provincial Deputy Provincial Administrator for Economic and Social Services, said the donation was aimed at supporting churches that have long filled service gaps where government presence is limited.

“Churches have played a vital role in rural areas by establishing schools, health facilities, roads, and most importantly, by preaching the Gospel,” he said. “In many places where government services have not reached, the church is already there.”

Kilip said the support would help strengthen night services, improve fellowship, and allow churches to continue spreading the Gospel more effectively.

“Partnership between churches, government, and the people is very important,” he said. “When these three work together, communities become peaceful, stable, and developed.”

He added that churches also play a key role in changing mindsets and promoting respect for law and order.

Pastor Dickson Nom of Topua PNG Bible Church thanked Mr. Kilip on behalf of the local churches, describing the assistance as the first of its kind in the area. He said the lack of lighting had been a real challenge for churches, and acknowledged that God had provided relief through Mr. Kilip’s support.

item.title: K3m spark for Vanimo Free Trade Zone, but massive infrastructure hurdles remain
item.description: A 20-year political promise moved a step closer to fruition as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone [FTZ] secured a K3 million operational startup fund from the Vanimo-Green District Development Authority [DDA]. Yet, despite local MP Belden Namah declaring the project a reality and no longer a dream, the ambitious trade hub faces a staggering checklist of missing critical infrastructure before it can succes
item.content:encoded:
A 20-year political promise moved a step closer to fruition as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone [FTZ] secured a K3 million operational startup fund from the Vanimo-Green District Development Authority [DDA].

Yet, despite local MP Belden Namah declaring the project a reality and no longer a dream, the ambitious trade hub faces a staggering checklist of missing critical infrastructure before it can successfully compete on the global stage.

The project, newly incorporated with the Investment Promotion Authority [IPA] as the Vanimo Free Trade Zone Holdings Limited, is designed to turn West Sepik into a manufacturing and logistics powerhouse by leveraging its proximity to neighboring Indonesia.

However, for the trade zone to achieve full operational capacity, Vanimo desperately lacks the basic foundations of an international trading hub. To attract foreign investment, the project requires an international-standard airport, an upgraded shipping wharf, a secure land mass for an export processing zone, and a streamlined, fully functional border post at Wutung.

Furthermore, the local manufacturing sector remains heavily handcuffed by PNG’s unstable provincial power and water grid. Minister for Tourism, Arts, and Culture, Belden Namah, who has led the project since its initial conception, acknowledged these gaps, stating he is aggressively lobbying the National Government to step in.

“I am pushing hard to the National Government for Vanimo to have an International Airport and an International Sea Port," Namah said. "I am also hoping that the National Government fast-tracks the rollout of the province having access to the excess power supply from Indonesia.”

First conceptualized over two decades ago by former West Sepik Governor John Tekwi, the economic zone has long been viewed as the country's best shot at diversifying away from resource extraction and into downstream manufacturing.

“This initiative has the potential to transform not only the Vanimo-Green electorate but the entire West Sepik Province," the minister said. "Let me assure you today, it is no longer a dream. The Vanimo Free Trade Zone is here to stay.”

While Namah credited the Marape-Rosso government's policy framework for creating the pathway to bring this long-term vision to life, locals say that the true test of the Vanimo FTZ will not be this initial K3 million startup capital. Instead, the survival of the project depends on whether Port Moresby will commit the hundreds of millions required to build the transport and utility networks needed to sustain it.

item.title: K120m waterfront overhaul to launch PNG fisheries to international standards
item.description: Papua New Guinea’s fisheries sector is on the brink of a massive economic and educational upgrade. National Minister for Fisheries and Marine Resources, Jelta Wong, officially broke ground yesterday on a K120 million redevelopment of the National Fisheries College [NFC] waterfront in Kavieng. The high-impact project is designed to elevate local training to strict international commercial standards
item.content:encoded:
Papua New Guinea’s fisheries sector is on the brink of a massive economic and educational upgrade. National Minister for Fisheries and Marine Resources, Jelta Wong, officially broke ground yesterday on a K120 million redevelopment of the National Fisheries College [NFC] waterfront in Kavieng.

The high-impact project is designed to elevate local training to strict international commercial standards.

The overhaul, funded by a K96 million grant from the Government of Japan’s Official Development Assistance [ODA] program and backed by K24 million in PNG Government counter-funding, directly targets critical gaps in the local maritime and fisheries workforce.

Once completed in the third quarter of 2028, the upgraded facility will allow the NFC to host world-class certifications right here in the country. This includes specialized commercial fishing operations [CFO] and refrigeration mechanics training, skills desperately needed to keep PNG competitive on the global stage.

The comprehensive infrastructure boost includes state-of-the-art onshore classrooms, an administrative office block, a dedicated wharf for the vetting of vessels, and a massive 38.5-meter dedicated training ship. The vessel will actively facilitate hands-on CFO and Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated [IUU] fishing counter-measures training.

"I am confident that the project of the NFC waterfront redevelopment will be implemented as scheduled for the benefit of our subsistence farmers, small-scale operators, and the general citizens of this country," Minister Wong said of the long-term economic ripple effects for local coastal communities.

Wong extended deep gratitude to the Japanese government on behalf of the state, stating that this project marks a milestone in a 51-year bilateral partnership. The relationship dates back to pre-independence 1974, when Japan built the college’s very first auditorium. He also commended the Managing Director of the National Fisheries Authority [NFA] and the Japan International Cooperation Agency [JICA] for their tireless collaborative efforts to clear the administrative hurdles and secure the project's launch.

The groundbreaking drew a heavy delegation of international and state dignitaries, including JICA Director General Yoneda Gen, First Secretary of the Embassy of Japan Takahashi Yoichiro, NFA Chairman Laurie Williams, and Department of National Planning and Monitoring Secretary Koney Samuel. Local stakeholders, town mayor Greg Toxie Seth, and provincial law enforcement heads also turned out in numbers alongside the Kavieng public to witness the historic launch.

However, the massive development milestone was also marked by a distinct political void. New Ireland Governor Byron Chan and Treasury Minister, who is also the Open Member for Kavieng, Ian Ling-Stuckey, were both notably absent from the ceremony.

The project is locked into a strict two-year implementation timeline, with major construction slated to run through to late 2028.

item.title: Police confiscate illicit alcohol in Mt Hagen
item.description: The growing sale and consumption of illicit alcohol in Mt Hagen city has become a major concern for police, with authorities blaming the illegal liquor for increasing social disorder and lawlessness in the province. Western Highlands police have now confiscated large quantities of illicit alcohol from several shops in Mt Hagen during the ongoing High Speed Police Visibility Operation.
item.content:encoded:
The growing sale and consumption of illicit alcohol in Mt Hagen city has become a major concern for police, with authorities blaming the illegal liquor for increasing social disorder and lawlessness in the province.

Western Highlands police have now confiscated large quantities of illicit alcohol from several shops in Mt Hagen during the ongoing High Speed Police Visibility Operation.

Provincial Police Commander Chief Superintendent John Sagom said the illegal alcohol is being sold cheaply in shops around the city and police have warned shop owners and distributors to stop selling the products immediately.

PPC Sagom said the illicit alcohol contains very high alcohol content, far above the legal six percent limit allowed in the country.

He said the strong alcohol content is causing consumers to become heavily intoxicated, leading to disorderly behaviour, violence and other law and order problems in Mt Hagen and surrounding communities.

“The alcohol being sold illegally is dangerous. Many people are becoming heavily intoxicated and causing trouble in the city and communities,” Sagom said.

He added that police alone cannot fight the issue and called on relevant authorities to work together in addressing the illegal sale and distribution of illicit alcohol in the province.

Sagom also warned that police will continue confiscating illicit alcohol and arresting those involved in the illegal business as part of efforts to restore peace and public safety in Mt Hagen city.

item.title: Young Ireng to represent PNG at Asia Pacific Amateur Championship
item.description: Young golf sensation, Jabez Ireng will be representing Papua New Guinea at the upcoming Asia Pacific Amateur Championship in New Zealand, from May 23- 30. The 15- year- old junior golf champion from the Madang Country Club will depart the country on Friday to participate in the week-long event with junior golfers from 14 other countries in the Asia- Pacific Region.
item.content:encoded:
Young golf sensation, Jabez Ireng will be representing Papua New Guinea at the upcoming Asia Pacific Amateur Championship in New Zealand, from May 23- 30.

The 15- year- old junior golf champion from the Madang Country Club will depart the country on Friday to participate in the week-long event with junior golfers from 14 other countries in the Asia- Pacific Region.

Jabez was the only junior golfer to be selected last year by the Augusta National Golf Club in Georgia, USA and will be representing Papua New Guinea in the championship.

Jabez's father, George Ireng, is a golfer himself and is happy with his son's achievement.

"This is something big for the Madang Country Club and for Madang Province because we've had no other juniors repping Madang in such tournaments. There had been representatives from other centers like Lae and Port Moresby but not Madang.

"This is his first overseas stint, and I am proud of him, and I am also escorting him just in case he gets lost along the way." George said.

Jabez has been playing golf for 8 years, and he is excited, looking forward to the event.

"I am happy to take the trip. I am also thankful to MadFox Pawa for the sponsorship," he said.

MadFox Pawa, a Madang- based company, has come onboard to support Jabez with his official uniform; two sets of polo shirts and caps to wear during the tournament.

MadFox Pawa Director, Gary Litz, said the company is excited to be doing something for junior golf in Madang.

"Since I found out a good friend of mine George Ireng went to play in Port Moresby and his son J Ireng winning the championship there, and is now representing PNG in New Zealand, I reached out to George and told him I wanted to support him and give him some uniform to represent our small company and even Madang.

"We always support our Madang youths in sports, but this is the first time we are supporting golf. We've supported basketball and soccer before.

"To Jabez, I am looking forward to the results, and I wish him all the best," Gary said.

Supporting sponsors besides MadFox Pawa are Sebiten Ltd, SBS Electrical and Noble Kava.

item.title: Coates to push for Kumuls debut at World Cup
item.description: Star rookie Phillip Coates will be lapping up any more NRL chances that come his way in 2026 as he pushes his case for a World Cup spot with the PNG Kumuls. The 19-year-old winger had a baptism of fire on debut for Brisbane Broncos in front of a parochial pro-Warriors crowd at NRL Magic Round, producing mixed results as he was blown away by the speed of the top grade.
item.content:encoded:
Star rookie Phillip Coates will be lapping up any more NRL chances that come his way in 2026 as he pushes his case for a World Cup spot with the PNG Kumuls.

The 19-year-old winger had a baptism of fire on debut for Brisbane Broncos in front of a parochial pro-Warriors crowd at NRL Magic Round, producing mixed results as he was blown away by the speed of the top grade.

But just as his older brother Xavier seems to do every game, Phil walked away with a highlights reel moment after latching on to a bullet-like Reece Walsh pass above his head, finding space and the saying his prayers as he lobbed an offload bouncing favourably into the arms of Adam Reynolds for the Broncos skipper's second try.

"It was very surreal. I missed out on a couple in the first half, so I needed to be better and I caught that one and that was a pretty special to set up Reyno there," Coates said.

"Lucky he was on the inside and scored the try, so it made me look good.

"It was an unlucky result but I'm very grateful to get my debut, especially in Magic Round. It was a great atmosphere, there were a lot of people, but it was good.

"It's very fast out there so compared to Q-Cup so in the first half it was a bit fast but I got into it in the second half and felt a bit better."

Coates' 25-year-old brother, who is one of the stars of the Melbourne Storm, flew to Brisbane to present his younger sibling's NRL debut jersey and the first-gamer said his advice had been invaluable.

"It was pretty special for him to present my jersey and he gave me a lot of good advice and I'm pretty grateful for having him in my corner," Phillip Coates said.

"He just said just keep it simple just keep doing what I'm doing You don't need to change anything because it's a big game so just act like it's a normal game.

"It's definitely inspirational. He's someone I can go to for advice and he's been through a lot for how old he is - he's only young but he's been through a lot - so he's always great with advice and I'm always asking him and he's giving me great advice, so I'll grateful for him."

One way Phil wants to emulate his brother is by representing Papua New Guinea, having got a taste for the meaning of the jersey while playing centre for the Junior Kumuls in 2024.

"It's been on my mind, so I would love to represent the Kumuls in the World Cup but I've just got to keep performing and doing well," Coates said.

"I'm just getting my confidence up and hopefully I can get a couple more [NRL] games in, but just really working on little things in Q-Cup and it would be good.

"The Kumuls play their opening two RLWC2026 matches at Santos National Stadium in Port Moresby against Lebanon and Samoa on October 17 and October 24.

The PNG side then travel to Wollongong to face England at WIN Stadium on October 30 in what is likely to be a must-win match for a place in the World Cup finals.

Coates may need to bide his time to add to his NRL tally, with the Broncos expected to welcome Kotoni Staggs after State of Origin Game 1 and the looming availability of Grant Anderson in coming weeks.

But if the former Queensland Under 19s representative continues his development and his impressive record of seven tries in six games for Burleigh Bears, a place in PNG's World Cup squad may be within reach.

item.title: Suspect shot by accomplices following Manu armed robbery
item.description: A robbery suspect is in custody after being accidentally shot by his own accomplices following a heist at the Manu Cash and Carry store this afternoon. The incident occurred around 2:00pm, prompting a swift response from police that forced three other armed suspects to flee the scene in a waiting getaway vehicle.
item.content:encoded:
A robbery suspect is in custody after being accidentally shot by his own accomplices following a heist at the Manu Cash and Carry store this afternoon.

The incident occurred around 2:00pm, prompting a swift response from police that forced three other armed suspects to flee the scene in a waiting getaway vehicle.

According to authorities, the wounded suspect was apprehended by members of the public while attempting to escape. A struggle ensued as the suspect fought those holding him down. In a desperate bid to free him, one of his accomplices opened fire, accidentally shooting the suspect instead.

Two local taxi drivers who were at the scene were also injured by the armed men during the commotion. Following the shooting, the remaining criminals scrambled into their getaway car and fled the area.

NCD/Central Commander Benjamin Turi commended the quick response of the police officers, noting that the wounded suspect was actively trying to evade capture when the shooting occurred.

Commander Turi stated that investigators expect to identify and apprehend the remaining suspects currently on the run, leveraging interviews with the wounded suspect and a thorough review of CCTV footage from the shop.

Police records indicate a recent spike in armed robberies and car thefts across the National Capital District in recent weeks. In response, police are intensifying operations to track down and prosecute those involved in the criminal upsurge.

Meanwhile, Commander Turi issued a stern warning to the public to give way to police vehicles during emergency responses.

"When police are responding to such cases, the public must make way so officers can maintain visual contact with fleeing criminals and carry out their duties effectively," Turi said. "You risk becoming a victim of crossfire if a shootout occurs between police and criminals."

item.title: PPC Sagom condemns viral attack on women
item.description: The Western Highlands Police Commander Chief Superintendent John Sagom has strongly condemned a group of men allegedly going around the city of Mt Hagen cutting ladies’ trousers and hair extensions. The incident has gone viral on all forms of social media, with some claims accusing police and members of the Kumul 23 Force of being involved.
item.content:encoded:
The Western Highlands Police Commander Chief Superintendent John Sagom has strongly condemned a group of men allegedly going around the city of Mt Hagen cutting ladies’ trousers and hair extensions.

The incident has gone viral on all forms of social media, with some claims accusing police and members of the Kumul 23 Force of being involved.

However, Western Highlands PPC Sagom said police have never approved, encouraged, or sanctioned such actions.

He said the acts were carried out by an isolated group and not by members of the police force or Kumul 23, as widely alleged by the public.

Sagom said police are not living in the Stone Age and so as the people and will never tolerate officers harassing women in public.

The PPC said the actions are degrading, unlawful, and damaging to the integrity of the police force.

He said police in Western Highlands and Kumul 23 personnel have no authority to go around attacking or humiliating women in the city.

Meanwhile, Upper Highlands Special Operations Commander Anthony Wagambie Jnr has also condemned the actions and is now working closely with PPC Sagom to identify those responsible.

PPC Sagom said investigations are continuing, and police have already brought in several suspected vehicles to the Mt Hagen Police Station, which may have been used by the group involved in the incidents.

Anyone responsible will be dealt with according to the law.

The Western Highlands Police Chief is now calling on the public not to fear or believe false information circulating on social media, assuring the community that Hagen police will never support such behavior under his command.

item.title: Police probe Mt. Hagen harassment, special operations deny involvement
item.description: Assistant Commissioner Anthony Wagambie Jr., Commander of the Upper Highlands Special Operations (UHSOPS), has confirmed that vehicles reportedly used to harass women in Mt. Hagen do not belong to the Special Operations fleet. Wagambie said all police vehicle and personnel movements are strictly task oriented and monitored, and eyewitness descriptions did not match any vehicles under UHSOPS.
item.content:encoded:
Assistant Commissioner Anthony Wagambie Jr., Commander of the Upper Highlands Special Operations (UHSOPS), has confirmed that vehicles reportedly used to harass women in Mt. Hagen do not belong to the Special Operations fleet.

Wagambie said all police vehicle and personnel movements are strictly task oriented and monitored, and eyewitness descriptions did not match any vehicles under UHSOPS.

He condemned the behavior as “unbecoming,” stressing that no law enforcement officer would dictate how women dress in this day and age.

The Western Highlands Provincial Police Commander, Chief Superintendent John Sagom, has been tasked to investigate swiftly and thoroughly.

Wagambie pledged UHSOPS support if required.

He also warned of false social media claims designed to destabilize operations, noting that recent posts implicating Kumul 23 were “false and misleading.”

“Mt. Hagen is the Highlands capital, where people from Jiwaka, Simbu, and beyond gather for services and work. Police and military personnel are stationed across these provinces, and we must ensure trust is maintained,” Wagambie said.

Western Highlands Police, under PPC Sagom’s leadership, have built strong community partnerships. Wagambie emphasized that WHP Police are working to identify the culprits and deal with them, while UHSOPS will continue to support efforts to uphold public confidence.
